summary: No-ship: the money-facing list-net seam is still structurally weak, and the public nullable fallback is not pinned at the route seam.

findings.title: Trip net port loses the membership dimension
findings.body: The skeleton seam accepts many `(tripId, memberId)` pairs but returns `Map<tripId, net>`, and the implementation writes each result with `out.set(tripId, ...)`. That cannot represent two members for the same trip; the later pair overwrites the earlier. The current GET /trips caller likely sends unique trip IDs, but that invariant is not encoded in the signature, so any future reuse for member-level net views would silently report wrong balances instead of failing fast.
findings.recommendation: Either narrow the port to a trip-unique request and assert duplicates, or return results per requested membership, e.g. an array aligned to input order or a map keyed by both tripId and memberId; compute settlement once per trip and project all requested members from it.

findings.title: Test-quality: Seam discipline
findings.body: The I-4 controller test block covers member id, user_id absence, zero/no-activity, and signed nets, but it does not pin the PRD-recorded corrupt-trip -> `my_net_amount: null` behavior at the public GET /trips seam. That nullable DTO mapping lives in the controller, while the corrupt-trip assertion is only below it in SettlementsService tests; a route refactor could accidentally turn null into '0' and this skeleton's public-contract tests would still pass.
findings.recommendation: Add a GET /trips controller test with one healthy trip and one corrupt included expense trip, asserting the healthy item keeps its signed net and only the corrupt item's `my_net_amount` is null.

next_steps: Fix the net lookup result shape or enforce trip-unique input at the port boundary.
Add the missing public route seam test for corrupt-trip null behavior.
